name: France decriminalises sodomy
description: Following the French Revolution, France becomes the first country in Europe to decriminalise same-sex sexual relations when the new penal code omits sodomy as a crime. The Napoleonic Code of 1810 later extended this across French-controlled territories.
significance: The first legal decriminalisation of homosexuality in a major European nation, influencing later European legal reforms.

name: Karl Heinrich Ulrichs publishes 'Uranian' theory
description: German lawyer Karl Heinrich Ulrichs publishes the first in a series of pamphlets arguing that 'Uranians' (people with same-sex attraction) are a natural third sex and should not be criminalised. He publicly defended same-sex love before the Congress of German Jurists in 1867, believed to be the first public gay rights speech in history.
significance: Pioneering the scientific and rights-based framework for discussing homosexuality, Ulrichs is often called the first gay rights activist.

name: Scientific-Humanitarian Committee founded
description: Magnus Hirschfeld, Max Spohr, and colleagues found the Wissenschaftlich-humanitäres Komitee (Scientific-Humanitarian Committee) in Berlin — the world's first gay rights organization. It campaigned to repeal Paragraph 175, which criminalised homosexuality in Germany.
significance: The world's first sustained gay rights advocacy organization, establishing the model of science-based advocacy.

name: Institut für Sexualwissenschaft opens
description: Magnus Hirschfeld opens the Institut für Sexualwissenschaft (Institute for Sexual Science) in Berlin — the world's first sexology research institute. It offered gender-affirming care, housed the first known trans community, and conducted pioneering research on homosexuality and gender diversity.
significance: The first trans healthcare center and LGBTQ+ research institution in the world, destroyed by Nazis in 1933.

name: Nazi raid on Institut für Sexualwissenschaft
description: Students from the Nazi German Student Union raid Hirschfeld's Institut für Sexualwissenschaft, destroying its library of over 20,000 books and 35,000 photographs in a public book-burning four days later. The institute was permanently shut down.
significance: Marked the beginning of systematic Nazi persecution of LGBTQ+ people; the images of burning books became iconic symbols of fascist censorship.

name: Gay concentration camp survivors denied recognition
description: At the end of World War II, gay men who survived Nazi concentration camps were not recognised as victims by Allied forces or post-war German governments. Many were re-imprisoned under Paragraph 175 directly from the camps. Full recognition and apologies only came decades later.
significance: The ongoing criminalisation after liberation highlighted the systemic erasure of gay suffering during the Holocaust.

name: Kinsey Scale published
description: Alfred Kinsey publishes 'Sexual Behavior in the Human Male', introducing the Kinsey Scale — a 0–6 spectrum of sexual orientation. The study found that 37% of American men had some homosexual experience and challenged the binary view of sexuality.
significance: The first large-scale scientific evidence challenging the binary view of sexuality, laying groundwork for later LGBTQ+ rights arguments.

name: Alan Turing charged with gross indecency
description: Computing pioneer Alan Turing, whose work was instrumental in breaking the Enigma code in WWII, is charged with 'gross indecency' for his relationship with another man. He accepts chemical castration (hormone treatment) as an alternative to prison. He died in 1954 in circumstances ruled a suicide.
significance: Turing's prosecution became a defining symbol of homophobic persecution; he received a royal pardon in 2013.

name: Mattachine Society founded in LA
description: Harry Hay founds the Mattachine Society in Los Angeles — one of the earliest sustained gay rights organizations in the United States. The group provided a social network and early advocacy for gay rights at a time of intense police repression and McCarthyite persecution.
significance: One of the first American homophile organizations, laying the groundwork for the LGBTQ+ rights movement.

name: Daughters of Bilitis founded
description: Del Martin and Phyllis Lyon found the Daughters of Bilitis in San Francisco — the first lesbian civil and political rights organization in the United States. It published 'The Ladder', the first lesbian publication in the US.
significance: The first lesbian rights organization in the US, providing community and advocacy at a time of extreme social repression.

name: Wolfenden Report recommends decriminalization in UK
description: The Wolfenden Committee report recommends that homosexual behaviour between consenting adults in private should no longer be a criminal offence in England and Wales. It took a further 10 years for Parliament to act on the recommendation.
significance: A landmark moment in British legal history, articulating the principle that the state should not legislate private morality.

name: Compton's Cafeteria Riot
description: Transgender women, drag queens, and gay people riot at Compton's Cafeteria in the Tenderloin district of San Francisco after police harass patrons. This is one of the first known instances of collective resistance against police harassment of LGBTQ+ people in the US.
significance: One of the first documented LGBTQ+ riots in the US, pre-dating Stonewall; particularly significant for trans history.

name: Sexual Offences Act 1967 (England and Wales)
description: The Sexual Offences Act 1967 partially decriminalises homosexual acts between men in England and Wales, but only in private, only for those 21 and over, and only for two people. Scotland and Northern Ireland are not included (they follow in 1980 and 1982 respectively).
significance: A major legal milestone in the UK, though decriminalisation remained partial and unequal for decades.

name: Stonewall Uprising
description: In the early morning of June 28, patrons of the Stonewall Inn on Christopher Street in Greenwich Village resist a police raid. Multi-night riots follow, led largely by trans women of color including Marsha P. Johnson and Sylvia Rivera. The uprising catalyses the modern LGBTQ+ rights movement.
significance: The pivotal event of the modern LGBTQ+ rights movement; June 28 is now observed as the anniversary of Pride.

name: First Pride Marches
location: New York, Los Angeles, San Francisco, Chicago, USA
description: On the first anniversary of Stonewall, the Christopher Street Liberation Day march takes place in New York City alongside simultaneous marches in Los Angeles, San Francisco, and Chicago. These are the first Pride marches in history.
significance: The birth of Pride as an annual public demonstration; the template for thousands of Pride events held globally today.

name: First European Pride (Amsterdam)
description: The first European Gay Pride march is held in Amsterdam, Netherlands. The Netherlands had already decriminalised homosexuality in 1811 under French rule, and Amsterdam became an early centre of LGBTQ+ culture in Europe.
significance: The start of Pride in Europe; Amsterdam remains a landmark city for LGBTQ+ rights.

name: APA removes homosexuality from DSM
description: The American Psychiatric Association votes to remove homosexuality from the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-II), ending its official classification as a mental illness. This followed years of activist pressure including disrupting APA conferences.
significance: A watershed moment in destigmatising homosexuality, removing the medical basis for pathologising LGBTQ+ people.

name: Gilbert Baker designs the Rainbow Flag
description: Gilbert Baker designs and flies the first rainbow flag at the San Francisco Gay Freedom Day Parade on June 25. The original 8-stripe design includes hot pink (sexuality), red (life), orange (healing), yellow (sunlight), green (nature), turquoise (magic/art), indigo (serenity), and violet (spirit).
significance: The rainbow flag became the defining symbol of LGBTQ+ identity worldwide.

name: First National March on Washington for Lesbian and Gay Rights
description: Approximately 75,000–100,000 people march on Washington D.C. demanding equal civil rights and an end to discrimination. It was the first major national LGBTQ+ demonstration in US history.
significance: Demonstrated the scale and political ambition of the post-Stonewall LGBTQ+ movement on a national stage.

name: First CDC report on AIDS
description: The US Centers for Disease Control publishes the first report on what would become known as AIDS, describing five cases of pneumocystis pneumonia in young gay men in Los Angeles. June 5 is now observed as HIV Long-Term Survivors Awareness Day.
significance: The formal beginning of the AIDS epidemic, which would kill over 700,000 Americans and devastate the LGBTQ+ community.

name: ACT UP founded
description: The AIDS Coalition to Unleash Power (ACT UP) is founded at the Lesbian and Gay Community Services Center in New York. It employs confrontational direct action to demand faster government response to the AIDS crisis, faster drug approval, and affordable treatment.
significance: ACT UP's tactics of direct action revolutionised patient advocacy and shaped drug approval processes globally.

name: Second National March on Washington; AIDS Memorial Quilt
description: Over 500,000 people march on Washington; the AIDS Memorial Quilt is displayed on the National Mall for the first time, covering an area larger than a football field with 1,920 panels commemorating people who died of AIDS.
significance: One of the largest LGBTQ+ demonstrations in US history; the Quilt became one of the most powerful memorials in American history.

name: Denmark legalises same-sex registered partnerships
description: Denmark becomes the first country in the world to legally recognise same-sex partnerships, granting most of the rights of marriage under the Registered Partnership Act. Same-sex marriage (with full equality) followed in 2012.
significance: The world's first legal recognition of same-sex partnerships — a historic milestone that inspired similar legislation worldwide.

name: WHO removes homosexuality from ICD
description: The World Health Organisation removes homosexuality from the International Classification of Diseases (ICD-10). May 17 is now observed annually as the International Day Against Homophobia, Biphobia and Transphobia (IDAHOBIT).
significance: Global health authority removal of homosexuality from the disease classification; the date now marks IDAHOBIT worldwide.

name: Irish decriminalisation
description: Ireland decriminalises homosexuality following a ruling by the European Court of Human Rights in Norris v. Ireland (1988), which found Ireland's laws violated Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights.
significance: The last major Western European country to decriminalise homosexuality; significant for the country's strong Catholic culture.

name: March on Washington for Lesbian, Gay and Bi Equal Rights
description: An estimated 800,000 to 1 million people march on Washington, making it one of the largest marches in US history. It included the first large-scale bi and trans visibility at a national march. The Quilt was displayed for the last time in its entirety.
significance: Demonstrated the enormous scale of LGBTQ+ political mobilisation; pivotal for bisexual and trans visibility.

name: Minnesota Human Rights Act amended — first trans protection in US
description: Minnesota amends its Human Rights Act to prohibit discrimination based on 'sexual orientation', including 'having or being perceived as having a self-image or identity not traditionally associated with one's biological maleness or femaleness'. This is the first explicit state-level legal protection for transgender people in the United States.
significance: The first US state-level statutory protection specifically covering transgender people.

name: Stonewall 25 — First World Pride
description: A massive march on the 25th anniversary of Stonewall draws over one million participants in New York City, alongside international marches in London, Paris, and Sydney. The event is considered the first 'World Pride'.
significance: The largest LGBTQ+ event in history to that date; crystallised Pride as a global phenomenon.

name: Murder of Matthew Shepard
description: 21-year-old gay college student Matthew Shepard is beaten, tortured, and tied to a fence outside Laramie, Wyoming. He dies in hospital six days later. The case drew international attention to anti-gay hate crimes and eventually led to the Matthew Shepard and James Byrd Jr. Hate Crimes Prevention Act (2009).
significance: Galvanised the push for federal hate crime legislation in the US and became a defining moment in LGBTQ+ advocacy.

name: Netherlands legalises same-sex marriage
description: The Netherlands becomes the first country in the world to open civil marriage to same-sex couples, granting full legal equality. Four same-sex couples marry at midnight in Amsterdam on April 1, 2001.
significance: The world's first country to grant full marriage equality — a watershed that opened the path for dozens of nations.

name: Lawrence v. Texas — US Supreme Court
description: The US Supreme Court rules 6–3 in Lawrence v. Texas that laws criminalising consensual same-sex sexual activity between adults are unconstitutional, striking down sodomy laws in 14 states. The majority opinion references the right to dignity and privacy.
significance: Decriminalised homosexuality across the United States, overturning Bowers v. Hardwick (1986).

name: Massachusetts legalises same-sex marriage — first US state
description: The Massachusetts Supreme Judicial Court rules in Goodridge v. Department of Public Health that denying marriage licenses to same-sex couples is unconstitutional. Massachusetts begins issuing marriage licenses to same-sex couples on May 17, 2004, becoming the first US state to do so.
significance: The first US state to legalise same-sex marriage, triggering a nationwide movement toward marriage equality.

name: Del Martin and Phyllis Lyon marry
description: Del Martin, 87, and Phyllis Lyon, 84 — founders of the Daughters of Bilitis (1955) — are among the first same-sex couples to legally marry in California during the brief window before Proposition 8. Del Martin dies 24 days later.
significance: A deeply symbolic moment — the pioneers of the US lesbian rights movement finally able to legally marry after 55 years together.

name: Matthew Shepard Act signed into law
description: President Obama signs the Matthew Shepard and James Byrd Jr. Hate Crimes Prevention Act, expanding federal hate crime protections to include sexual orientation, gender identity, gender, and disability. It was the first federal law to explicitly protect LGBTQ+ people.
significance: The first federal LGBTQ+-protective legislation in US history.

name: UN Human Rights Council adopts first LGBTQ+ rights resolution
description: The United Nations Human Rights Council adopts its first resolution on human rights, sexual orientation, and gender identity — Resolution 17/19. The vote was 23 in favour, 19 against, and 3 abstentions. It called for a study on violence and discrimination based on sexual orientation and gender identity.
significance: The first formal recognition by the UN Human Rights Council of LGBTQ+ rights as human rights.

name: US v. Windsor — DOMA struck down
description: The US Supreme Court rules 5–4 in United States v. Windsor that Section 3 of the Defense of Marriage Act (DOMA), which defined marriage as between a man and a woman for federal purposes, is unconstitutional. Edith Windsor had been required to pay $363,053 in estate taxes after her wife Thea Spyer died.
significance: Struck down DOMA and required the federal government to recognise same-sex marriages, a major step toward Obergefell.

name: Obergefell v. Hodges — marriage equality in USA
description: The US Supreme Court rules 5–4 in Obergefell v. Hodges that the Fourteenth Amendment requires states to license and recognise same-sex marriages, making marriage equality the law throughout the United States. Jim Obergefell sought recognition of his marriage to John Arthur, who had died of ALS.
significance: Marriage equality became federal law across all 50 US states — the culmination of decades of LGBTQ+ legal advocacy.

name: Taiwan Constitutional Court rules for marriage equality
description: Taiwan's Constitutional Court (Judicial Yuan Interpretation No. 748) rules that the Civil Code's restriction of marriage to opposite-sex couples is unconstitutional. Taiwan legalises same-sex marriage on May 24, 2019, becoming the first country in Asia to do so.
significance: The first marriage equality ruling in Asia, and a landmark for LGBTQ+ rights in a region where progress has been slower.

name: India decriminalises homosexuality — Section 377
description: The Supreme Court of India unanimously strikes down Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code, which had criminalised consensual same-sex relations since 1861. The ruling was a unanimous five-judge bench decision in Navtej Singh Johar v. Union of India.
significance: Decriminalisation in the world's most populous democracy and a major victory for South Asian LGBTQ+ communities.

name: Bostock v. Clayton County — Title VII protects LGBTQ+ workers
description: The US Supreme Court rules 6–3 in Bostock v. Clayton County that Title VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which prohibits employment discrimination 'because of sex', protects employees against discrimination based on sexual orientation and gender identity.
significance: Extended federal employment non-discrimination protections to LGBTQ+ people for the first time, covering over 11 million LGBTQ+ workers.

name: Respect for Marriage Act signed
description: President Biden signs the Respect for Marriage Act, which codifies federal recognition of same-sex and interracial marriages into statute. Passed in response to fears that Obergefell could be reversed following the Dobbs decision. Passed with bipartisan support.
significance: Federal statutory protection for same-sex marriage, providing a legislative backstop independent of the Supreme Court.

name: Greece legalises same-sex marriage
description: Greece becomes the first majority-Orthodox Christian country to legalise same-sex marriage, passing the bill through parliament despite strong opposition from the Greek Orthodox Church. The legislation also grants same-sex couples adoption rights.
significance: A milestone in a country with deep religious conservatism; the 37th country globally to legalise same-sex marriage.
